brands.description: Google Earth is a virtual globe, map and geographical information program that was originally called EarthViewer 3D created by Keyhole, Inc, a Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) funded company acquired by Google in 2004 (see In-Q-Tel). It maps the Earth by the superimposition of images obtained from satellite imagery, aerial photography and geographic information system (GIS) onto a 3D globe.
brands.name: Google Earth

brands.description: YouTube is a video-sharing website headquartered in San Bruno, California, United States. The service was created by three former PayPal employees in February 2005. In November 2006, it was bought by Google for US$1.65 billion. YouTube now operates as one of Google's subsidiaries.
brands.name: YouTube

brands.description: DNV GL is an international certification body and classification society with main expertise in technical assessment, advisory, and risk management. It was created in 2013 as a result of a merger between two leading organizations in the field-Det Norske Veritas (Norway) and Germanischer Lloyd (Germany).
brands.name: DNV GL

brands.description: Airbus SAS is a division of the multinational Airbus Group SE that manufactures civil aircraft. It is based in Blagnac, France, a suburb of Toulouse, with production and manufacturing facilities mainly in France, Germany, Spain, China, United Kingdom and the United States. The company employs 73,958.
brands.name: Airbus

brands.description: All Nippon Airways Co., Ltd. , also known as Zennikkū or ANA, is the largest airline in Japan. Its headquarters are located at Shiodome City Center in the Shiodome area of Minato, Tokyo, Japan. It operates services to both domestic and international destinations and had more than 20,000 employees as of March 2016.
brands.name: All Nippon Airways

brands.description: Samsung is a South Korean multinational conglomerate company headquartered in Samsung Town, Seoul. It comprises numerous subsidiaries and affiliated businesses, most of them united under the Samsung brand, and is the largest South Korean chaebol (business conglomerate).
brands.name: Samsung

namedLocations.description: Mumbai (/mʊmˈbaɪ/, also known as Bombay /bɒmˈbeɪ/, the official name until 1995) is the capital city of the Indian state of Maharashtra. As of 2011 it is the most populous city in India with an estimated city proper population of 12.4 million. The larger Mumbai Metropolitan Region is the second-most-populous metropolitan area in India, with a population of 21.3 million as of 2016. Mumbai lies on the Konkan coast on the west coast of India and has a deep natural harbour. In 2008, Mumbai was named an alpha world city. It is also the wealthiest city in India, and has the highest number of millionaires and billionaires among all cities in India. Mumbai is home to three UNESCO World Heritage Sites: the Elephanta Caves, Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Terminus, and the city's distinctive ensemble of Victorian and Art Deco buildings.
namedLocations.name: Mumbai

namedLocations.description: Navi Mumbai ([nəʋiː mumbəiː], also known by its former name New Bombay), is a planned city off the west coast of the Indian state of Maharashtra in Konkan division. The city is divided into two parts, North Navi Mumbai and South Navi Mumbai, for the individual development of Panvel Mega City, which includes the area from Kharghar to Uran. Navi Mumbai has a population of 1,119,477 as per the 2011 provisional census.
namedLocations.name: Navi Mumbai

namedLocations.description: India (ISO: Bhārat), also known as the Republic of India (ISO: Bhārat Gaṇarājya), is a country in South Asia. It is the seventh-largest country by area, the second-most populous country, and the most populous democracy in the world. Bounded by the Indian Ocean on the south, the Arabian Sea on the southwest, and the Bay of Bengal on the southeast, it shares land borders with Pakistan to the west; China, Nepal, and Bhutan to the northeast; and Bangladesh and Myanmar to the east. In the Indian Ocean, India is in the vicinity of Sri Lanka and the Maldives; its Andaman and Nicobar Islands share a maritime border with Thailand and Indonesia.
namedLocations.name: India

namedLocations.description: Changi Airport Group was formed on 1 July 2009. It is the operator of Singapore Changi Airport and Singapore Seletar Airport. Its role was previously undertaken by the Civil Aviation Authority of Singapore. The group's head office is located at Terminal 2 of Changi Airport in Changi.
namedLocations.name: Singapore Changi Airport

namedLocations.description: Tokyo (東京 Tōkyō, English: /ˈtoʊkioʊ/, Japanese: [toːkʲoː] ; lit. "Eastern Capital"), officially Tokyo Metropolis (東京都 Tōkyō-to), one of the 47 prefectures of Japan, has served as the Japanese capital since 1869. As of 2018, the Greater Tokyo Area ranked as the most populous metropolitan area in the world. The urban area houses the seat of the Emperor of Japan, of the Japanese government and of the National Diet. Tokyo forms part of the Kantō region on the southeastern side of Japan's main island, Honshu, and includes the Izu Islands and Ogasawara Islands. Tokyo was formerly named Edo when Shōgun Tokugawa Ieyasu made the city his headquarters in 1603. It became the capital after Emperor Meiji moved his seat to the city from Kyoto in 1868; at that time Edo was renamed Tokyo. Tokyo Metropolis formed in 1943 from the merger of the former Tokyo Prefecture (東京府 Tōkyō-fu) and the city of Tokyo (東京市 Tōkyō-shi). Tokyo is often referred to as a city but is officially known and governed as a "metropolitan prefecture", which differs from and combines elements of a city and a prefecture, a characteristic unique to Tokyo.
namedLocations.name: Tokyo

namedLocations.description: Chhatrapati Shivaji International Airport (CSIA) (IATA: BOM, ICAO: VABB), formerly Sahar International Airport, is the primary international airport serving the Mumbai Metropolitan Area, India.
namedLocations.name: Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport

namedLocations.description: Mumbai Trans Harbour Link (MTHL), also known as Sewri-Nhava Sheva Trans Harbour Link, is a proposed 22 km, freeway grade road bridge connecting the Indian city of Mumbai with Navi Mumbai, its satellite city. When completed, it would be the longest sea bridge in India.
namedLocations.name: Mumbai Trans Harbour Link

namedLocations.description: The Indian subcontinent or the subcontinent is a southern region of Asia, mostly situated on the Indian Plate and projecting southwards into the Indian Ocean from the Himalayas. Definitions of the extent of the Indian subcontinent differ but it usually includes the core lands of India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh; Nepal, Bhutan, Sri Lanka, and the Maldives are often included as well.
namedLocations.name: Indian subcontinent

namedLocations.description: London Southend Airport (IATA: SEN, ICAO: EGMC) is an international airport in the district of Rochford within Essex, England, approximately 42 miles (68 km) from the centre of London. During the 1960s, Southend was London's third-busiest airport.
namedLocations.name: London Southend Airport

namedLocations.description: London Stansted Airport (IATA: STN, ICAO: EGSS) is an international airport located at Stansted Mountfitchet in the district of Uttlesford in Essex, 42 mi (68 km) northeast of Central London. London Stansted serves 200 destinations across Europe, Middle East and Africa. Stansted is a base for a number of major European low-cost carriers, being the largest base for low-cost airline Ryanair, with over 130 destinations served by the airline. In 2015 it was the fourth busiest airport in the United Kingdom after Heathrow, Gatwick and Manchester. Stansted's runway is also used by private companies such as the Harrods Aviation, Titan Airways and XJet terminals which are private ground handlers who are able to handle private flights, charter flights and state visits. STN also has a transit inside which helps people to travel to their designated gates and terminals.
namedLocations.name: London Stansted Airport

namedLocations.description: Heathrow Airport (IATA: LHR, ICAO: EGLL) is a major international airport in west London, England, UK. Heathrow is the busiest airport in the United Kingdom, busiest airport in Europe by passenger traffic, and sixth busiest airport in the world by total passenger traffic. In 2015, it handled a record 75 million passengers, a 2.2 percent increase from 2014.
namedLocations.name: Heathrow Airport

namedLocations.description: London City Airport (IATA: LCY, ICAO: EGLC) is an international airport in London, United Kingdom. It is located in the Royal Docks in the London Borough of Newham, approximately 6 NM (11 km; 6.9 mi) east of the City of London and a shorter distance east of Canary Wharf. These are the twin centres of London's financial industry, which is a major user of the airport. The airport was developed by the engineering company Mowlem in 1986–87. In 2016 it was bought by a Canadian-led consortium of Alberta Investment Management Corporation (AIMCo), OMERS, the Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan and Wren House Infrastructure Management of the Kuwait Investment Authority.
namedLocations.name: London City Airport

namedPeople.description: Became the 15th Prime Minister of India in May 2014 as a member of the Bharatiya Janata Party. He previously served as Chief Minister of the Indian state of Gujarat, becoming the longest-serving Chief Minister in Gujarat history.
namedPeople.name: Narendra Modi

topics.iptcName: arts, culture, entertainment and media/arts and entertainment/visual arts/architecture
topics.name: Architecture/Architecture

topics.iptcName: science and technology/technology and engineering/civil engineering
topics.name: Architecture/Architecture & Civil Engineering

topics.iptcName: economy, business and finance/economic sector/construction and property/heavy construction
topics.name: Real Estate/Construction

topics.iptcName: science and technology/technology and engineering/civil engineering
topics.name: Education/Engineering/Civil Engineering

transcript.text: This might just be Indias answer to Changi.
It's called Navi Mumbai International Airports, and it opened its terminals to passengers in December last year.
The place looks pretty stunning.
And that's just phase one.
Once the final phase is complete, Navi Mumbai will welcome an astounding 90 million passengers a year, 20 million more than Changi.
India wants this to become a hub for World Travel.
Now, you'd be right to raise an eyebrow to any newcomer being compared to Singapore's pride and joy.
That is arguably the best airport in the world.
But don't forget that Changi was a military air base long before it became a civilian facility.
The engineers behind Navi Mumbai could only dream of building on an air base.
Their reality was entirely different.
They were tasked with building an airport on a swamp covered in unstable mud flats, a giant hill, and a river flowing right through the middle of it.
While the renders of Navi Mumbai International look spectacular, this airport's location has been an absolute, absolute nightmare.
India has one of the fastest growing aviation sectors on the planets, behind only China and the US.
The number of operational airports in the country has doubled in the last decade as Prime Minister Narendra Modi looks set to overhaul India's travel infrastructure.
Mumbai, it's financial powerhouse, is under resourced.
A number of the world's most prominent cities feature multiple major airports, think London, New York or Tokyo.
And that's obviously crucial for visitor numbers, business trips and cargo.
Let's take London for example.
The city is serviced by 6 major airports.
All of those combined handled A whopping 177 million passengers in 2024.
Mumbai, however, has historically had just one major airports.
That would be like London having just Heathrow.
The city would miss out on nearly 100 million passengers every year.
And given that rapidly growing aviation sector in India, the country is desperate to improve capacity to its financial capital.
The answer authorities came up with was simple a second major airport in Mumbai.
But executing an idea of this scale is rarely as straightforward as having the epiphany itself.
The biggest problem faced by Mumbai's second big airport plan is experienced by major cities all around the world.
The lack of space.
Just take a look at Mumbai airport, which is already a capacity terminal.
One over here is going through a redevelopments, but there isn't exactly a lot of scope for expanding the footprints on every side.
There are densely packed communities that would be a nightmare to move, and it's not like they didn't try.
That evidence can be found over at Terminal 2.
The concourses here are asymmetrical.
The initial plan was for them to be the same length, but because of the challenges faced in moving communities on the airport boundary, the second concourse had to be redesigned.
It looks like it's been cut off, and that obviously limits the number of gates that are available.
Air traffic is also pretty restricted because of the intersecting runways.
The design stops planes landing and taking off on each strip at the same time.
And as we pointed out, it's not like there's a lot of space to build parallel runways elsewhere.
The result is that Mumbai is missing out on potentially millions of passengers and tons of cargo every year.
And the city's key airport really is crying out for help.
But clearly a major expansion is out of the question, as is constructing a massive new airport anywhere near the city centre.
So where do you go?
Well, after a 10 year search and then a further three years spent buying up land, officials ended up here.
It's about 40 kilometres away from the old commercial centre of Mumbai.
Over in Navi Mumbai, in what's become a growing trend over the last decade, the Navi Mumbai international sites is on the coasts.
That means it's built largely on reclaimed land, and that's required a lot of preparation.
Now, before we go any further with today's video, we need to stop everything because I've got some very big news that I am finally allowed to share.
Last year, Penguin Random House asked me if I'd like to write a book.
I said yes, I've written a book and here it is.
It is called Mega Builds 10 Colossal Construction Projects That Will Change Our World.
Over the last 10 years I have had a front row seat some of this planet's most incredible construction projects and they are all in here.
I've written about them.
I've gone deeper than I've ever gone before in any video story or any kind of like long form documentary.
This is behind the scenes, it's up close and personal.
It meets the teams, it's the human stories, it's the engineering in detail.
It's my personal experiences and some of the calamities I've had along the way getting up close with these projects.
I have really enjoyed writing this.
Some of my mates have been like oh nice, who?
Who ghost wrote that for you?
No one.
I can't afford a fancy ghost writer.
Me, I wrote this.
Every single word of this has come from my head, into my laptop and then into this book.
All for your reading pleasure.
Guys.
I never thought I could write a book but I have and obviously I'm biased but I actually kind of think it's quite good.
It is available to pre-order now or the link is below.
Pre ordering is incredibly important.
Basically my entire future happiness depends on you pre ordering this book.

So guys, if you love the B1M, if you love these stories, if you love learning more about construction in a down to earth way that's easy to understand and can be read by anyone, go and pre-order my book Mega Bills today.
Before any work could start on the foundations back in 2018, three key hurdles had to be tackled and each of them was pretty massive.
Ori Hill, swamplands and Ori River.
Let's start here, right in the middle of the site for the new airport.
You'd find this all we hill.
It's 92 meters of solid rock.
Obviously, having a hill in the middle of your concourse isn't exactly ideal, so that had to be completely flattened before any building could take place.
Construction crews demolished 62 million cube meters of rock through controlled blasting to protect surrounding villages.
But once that was finished, the site looks like this.
So on to problem #2 what do you do with the blasted rock?
Well, it was repurposed.
You see, hundreds of acres of this airport sits on swampland.
It's covered in mud flats and mangroves, all of which have got to be leveled and stabilized before construction work.
And begin To do that, they had to raise the ground by around 6 meters, and conveniently, they had enormous amounts of rock available from that big old hill they've just blown up.
What's really cool is that these guys blasted the rock at specific explosive power designations to create rock fragments that were the ideal size for land development and reclamation.
The blasted rock was basically dumped into marshy areas, puncturing and displacing the soft clay.
The weight of all that rock then compacted the soil, making it stronger and reducing the amount of intensive treatment that was needed to thicken any weak ground.
But that wasn't the final hurdle.
This is Alwa River in 2019.
Originally it cut through the middle of the site, but beyond acting as an interesting water feature, a river through a runway is actually incredibly impractical.
Engineers were left with no choice but to redirect it.
You can see it's been cast at an angle diverting around the sites and if you're thinking it now looks bigger, then you'd be right.
Navi Mumbai airports is surrounded by villages whose people have watched this site completely transform.
But within those communities there's a very real concern about flooding.
You see swamp and wetlands absorb water through the vegetation and soft soil.
But as we pointed out, large swathes of that land have been filled in with a non absorbent rock.
Living villages wondering where water will run during heavy rainfall.
And that's why all we river has been expanded and deepened.
In some areas, it's grown from 25 meters wide to an impressive 200 meters wide.
The hope is that the larger area is going to control excess water during floods.
And just before we move on, remember that hill that had to be flattened?
Well, that wasn't the only thing that he'd taken back down to ground level.
Overhead tension cables had to be rerouted underground to stop airplanes from flying into them.
Now, after seven years of construction, the first phase of the airport opened on Christmas Day 2025, featuring a control tower, a single runway, a fire station, and Terminal 1.
Already, Navi Mumbai is able to serve 20 million passengers a year and half a million metric tons of cargo.
But by the end of phase five in the 20 thirties, this airport's going to be unrecognizable.
The site's being enhanced with a second parallel runway, 3, more terminals, and additional cargo capacity.
The plans will see Navi Mumbai International eventually processing a whopping 90 million passengers and more than 3,000,000 tons of cargo every year.
And it's pretty breathtaking to look at.
Designed by Zaha Hadid Architects, it's models to resemble a Lotus flower, the national flower of India.
The foundations are being developed using high strength reinforced concrete and precast panels to try and speed up the construction process.
The whole thing is a really impressive accomplishment given how incredibly impractical this site was at the start of the project.
At the front, 12 columns shaped like unfolding petals diffuse light into the terminal as part of a striking 2 tier system.
17 giant columns are then hidden behind them to take the incredible weights of the 370 meter canopy roof.
That curved roof is created using a steel framework supported by a network of steel girders and cladding.
Each action acts like a petal, formed using large span rectangular steel trusses.
Those trusses are able to carry a mass, massive amount of weight, and the result is the ability to reduce the number of columns inside the building.
The goal was to create a beautiful open space with natural light flooding in through the skylights, and I think it's fair to say mission accomplished.
But those holes in the roof are about a lot more than just giving passengers something nice to look at.
They actually catch and channel monsoon rainwater and help reduce wind resistance altogether.
They're incredibly practical.
And actually, I just want to touch on the site location one more time because sure, we've already shown you how far this is from the city.
And yes, moving away from the hustle and bustle means you have more room to play with.
That's understandable.
But what makes less sense is the very limited travel infrastructure that now leads to this open and active airport.
A new road network has been constructed, but for the time being there's no rail access.
There are reports that the mobile connectivity in the area is pretty poor, so communicating with app based transport systems is difficult and the taxi system is facing delays.
Now there are plans for a new metro line between the old airport and the new Navi Mumbai International, but that's not going to be ready for a number of years.
The 1 silver lining is the impressive Trans Harbour Link, the country's longest sea bridge, which opened in 2024 and connects Mumbai over to Navi Mumbai.
But the journey to the new airport by car can take up to three hours from some of the suburbs, and that's the last thing you need when you're about to hop on a long haul flight.
Now, Navi Mumbai isn't the first airport to face teething issues after opening, and it absolutely will not be the last.
But I can't help feeling that after Mumbai waited so long for a new airport, decades for this new airport to happen, the execution of this projects just feels a little bit rushed.
The search for a site LED them to somewhere that was covered in swampland with a hill and a river flowing through it.
And now the airport's opened, but without any rail access.
And let's be clear, we're still in Phase 1 of this project.
Navy Mumbai International has an incredibly exciting future.
To me, it looks a lot like one of the world's fastest growing aviation economies could be about to reach new heights.
Don't forget that we're raising awareness of constructions, mental health crisis and supporting charities in this space through our Get Construction Talking initiative.
You can learn more and find links to supports over at Get constructiontalking.org.
And as always guys, if you enjoyed this video and you want to get more from the definitive video channel for construction, make sure you're subscribed to the B1M.

namedLocations.description: Chhatrapati Shivaji International Airport (CSIA) (IATA: BOM, ICAO: VABB), formerly Sahar International Airport, is the primary international airport serving the Mumbai Metropolitan Area, India.

namedPeople.name: Narendra Modi
namedPeople.description: Became the 15th Prime Minister of India in May 2014 as a member of the Bharatiya Janata Party. He previously served as Chief Minister of the Indian state of Gujarat, becoming the longest-serving Chief Minister in Gujarat history.
